parts.title: Part 1 — Registering with a health centre
parts.scenario: A new patient phones the Elmwood Health Centre to register and book a first appointment.
parts.transcript:
Receptionist: Good morning, Elmwood Health Centre, Fiona speaking.
Patient: Hello, I've recently moved here and I'd like to register as a new patient.
Receptionist: Of course. Can I take your name?
Patient: Yes, it's Andrew Castellano. The surname is C-A-S-T-E-L-L-A-N-O.
Receptionist: Thank you. And your date of birth?
Patient: The twelfth of September, 1994.
Receptionist: Great. Do you have any ongoing medical conditions we should know about?
Patient: I'm asthmatic, so I use an inhaler, but otherwise I'm fine.
Receptionist: Noted. We'll need you to complete a registration form. You can do it online, or pick one up from reception. Now, would you like to book a health check with the practice nurse?
Patient: Yes please. When's the earliest?
Receptionist: The nurse, Sarah, has an opening next Wednesday at half past ten.
Patient: Wednesday at ten thirty, that works.
Receptionist: Please bring a list of any medication you take, and arrive ten minutes early to give a urine sample. Also, the health centre has moved since our website was last updated — we're now on Fenwick Road, opposite the primary school.
Patient: Fenwick Road, opposite the school. Is there parking?
Receptionist: There's a small car park behind the building, but it fills quickly, so the bus is often easier. The number 14 stops right outside.
Patient: Perfect. Thanks, Fiona.
Receptionist: You're welcome. See you Wednesday.

questions.explanation: Spelled out: 'C-A-S-T-E-L-L-A-N-O'.

questions.explanation: 'The twelfth of September, 1994'.

questions.explanation: 'I'm asthmatic, so I use an inhaler'.

questions.explanation: 'book a health check with the practice nurse'.

questions.explanation: 'next Wednesday at half past ten' — confirmed as 'ten thirty'.

questions.explanation: 'arrive ten minutes early to give a urine sample'.

questions.explanation: 'we're now on Fenwick Road, opposite the primary school'.

questions.explanation: 'The number 14 stops right outside'.

parts.title: Part 2 — Radio feature on a community walking scheme
parts.scenario: A presenter describes a local 'walk for health' scheme and how to take part.
parts.transcript: If you've been meaning to get more active but find the gym off-putting, our town's Walk for Health scheme might be just what you need. It began five years ago with a single group of eight people; today, more than four hundred residents take part each week. The idea is simple: free, friendly, volunteer-led walks suitable for all fitness levels. There are three routes to choose from. The Green route is the gentlest — a flat, thirty-minute stroll around the boating lake, ideal for beginners or anyone returning to exercise after illness. The Blue route is a brisker forty-five-minute walk through the woods, with a few gentle slopes. And the Red route is for the more confident: a challenging hour-long hike up to the ridge, with some steep sections. All walks start from the visitor centre car park. The Green and Blue walks run every morning; the Red route is Saturdays only. You don't need to book — just turn up in comfortable shoes about ten minutes before the start. Water is provided, but do bring a waterproof, as walks go ahead in all but the most extreme weather. One tip from our walk leaders: if you have a health condition, it's worth a quick word with your doctor before joining the Red route, though the gentler walks are safe for almost everyone. Many walkers tell us the biggest benefit isn't the exercise at all — it's the company. Friendships formed on these walks have, in several cases, lasted for years.

questions.question: How many people took part when the scheme began?
questions.explanation: 'It began five years ago with a single group of eight people'.

questions.question: Roughly how many residents now take part each week?
questions.explanation: 'more than four hundred residents take part each week'.

questions.question: The walks are led by:
questions.explanation: 'free, friendly, volunteer-led walks'.

questions.question: Which route is described as the gentlest?
questions.explanation: 'The Green route is the gentlest — a flat, thirty-minute stroll'.

questions.question: The Green route goes around the ______.
questions.explanation: 'a flat, thirty-minute stroll around the boating lake'.

questions.question: How long does the Blue route take?
questions.explanation: 'The Blue route is a brisker forty-five-minute walk through the woods'.

questions.question: The Red route runs on which day only?
questions.explanation: 'the Red route is Saturdays only'.

questions.question: All walks start from the visitor centre:
questions.explanation: 'All walks start from the visitor centre car park'.

questions.question: Walkers are advised to bring a ______ in case of rain.
questions.explanation: 'do bring a waterproof, as walks go ahead in all but the most extreme weather'.

questions.question: According to the presenter, the biggest benefit walkers report is:
questions.explanation: 'the biggest benefit isn't the exercise at all — it's the company'.

parts.title: Part 3 — Students planning a public health study
parts.scenario: Two students, Leo and Hana, discuss their study on sleep and student performance with their supervisor.
parts.transcript:
Supervisor: So, you're looking at sleep and academic performance. How's the design coming along?
Leo: We've decided to survey first-year students about their sleep habits and compare that with their exam results.
Hana: The tricky part is that self-reported sleep is unreliable — people tend to overestimate how much they sleep.
Supervisor: That's a real limitation. Have you considered any objective measures?
Hana: We looked into sleep-tracking wristbands, but they're too expensive to give to a large sample.
Leo: So we're compromising: a detailed sleep diary kept over two weeks, which is more accurate than a one-off estimate.
Supervisor: Sensible. Now, what's your hypothesis?
Leo: That students who sleep less than six hours perform worse. But we're aware correlation isn't causation — anxious students might both sleep badly and study poorly.
Supervisor: Exactly. You'll need to acknowledge confounding factors. What about your sample size?
Hana: We're aiming for two hundred students, though we expect a drop-out rate, so we'll recruit more to be safe.
Supervisor: Good thinking. And ethics — have you got approval?
Hana: We've submitted the form. The main concern was anonymity, so we're using ID codes rather than names.
Supervisor: Perfect. One more thing: think carefully about how you'll present the results. A clear graph often communicates more than a table full of numbers.
Leo: We were planning a scatter plot of sleep hours against average marks.
Supervisor: Ideal for showing a relationship. Just remember to state the strength of the correlation, not just its direction.

questions.question: The students will compare sleep habits with students':
questions.explanation: 'compare that with their exam results'.

questions.question: According to Hana, people tend to ______ how much they sleep.
questions.explanation: 'people tend to overestimate how much they sleep'.

questions.question: Why did they reject sleep-tracking wristbands?
options: too expensive for a large sample

questions.explanation: 'they're too expensive to give to a large sample'.

questions.explanation: 'a detailed sleep diary kept over two weeks'.

questions.explanation: 'students who sleep less than six hours perform worse'.

questions.explanation: 'we're aware correlation isn't causation'.

questions.explanation: 'We're aiming for two hundred students'.

questions.explanation: 'we're using ID codes rather than names'.

questions.question: The supervisor suggests presenting results as a:
questions.explanation: The students plan and the supervisor endorses 'a scatter plot of sleep hours against average marks'.

questions.question: The supervisor reminds them to state the ______ of the correlation, not just its direction.
questions.explanation: 'state the strength of the correlation, not just its direction'.

parts.title: Part 4 — Lecture on the history of vaccination
parts.scenario: A lecturer outlines how vaccination developed from folk practice to modern science.
parts.transcript: The story of vaccination is often told as beginning with a single experiment in 1796, but its roots go back much further. For centuries, people in China, India, and the Ottoman Empire practised a technique called variolation — deliberately exposing healthy people to material from smallpox sores, in the hope of provoking a mild infection that would grant lifelong protection. It was risky: a small proportion of those treated died. Yet the death rate was far lower than that of natural smallpox, so the practice spread. The technique reached Britain in the early eighteenth century, championed by Lady Mary Wortley Montagu, who had seen it in Constantinople. The decisive advance came from the English doctor Edward Jenner. He noticed that milkmaids who had caught cowpox, a mild disease, seemed immune to smallpox. In 1796, he tested the idea by deliberately infecting a boy with cowpox, and then exposing him to smallpox. The boy did not fall ill. Jenner called his method vaccination, from the Latin for cow. Crucially, it was far safer than variolation, because cowpox itself was rarely dangerous. For much of the nineteenth century, vaccination remained limited to smallpox, and no one understood why it worked. That understanding came only with the germ theory of disease, developed by Louis Pasteur and others, which revealed that infections were caused by micro-organisms and that the immune system could be trained to recognise them. Pasteur extended the principle to other diseases, and the modern era of vaccines began. The ultimate triumph came in 1980, when smallpox — the very disease that had launched the whole enterprise — was declared eradicated worldwide, the first and so far only human disease to be wiped out by deliberate effort.

questions.question: The technique of deliberately exposing people to smallpox material was called:
questions.explanation: 'a technique called variolation — deliberately exposing healthy people to material from smallpox sores'.

questions.question: Compared with natural smallpox, the death rate from variolation was:
questions.explanation: 'the death rate was far lower than that of natural smallpox'.

questions.question: Who championed variolation in Britain after seeing it in Constantinople?
questions.explanation: 'championed by Lady Mary Wortley Montagu, who had seen it in Constantinople'.

questions.question: Edward Jenner noticed that milkmaids who had caught ______ seemed immune to smallpox.
questions.explanation: 'milkmaids who had caught cowpox... seemed immune to smallpox'.

questions.question: In which year did Jenner carry out his famous experiment?
questions.explanation: 'In 1796, he tested the idea by deliberately infecting a boy with cowpox'.

questions.question: The word 'vaccination' comes from the Latin for:
questions.explanation: 'Jenner called his method vaccination, from the Latin for cow'.

questions.question: Vaccination was safer than variolation because cowpox was rarely:
questions.explanation: 'it was far safer than variolation, because cowpox itself was rarely dangerous'.

questions.question: Understanding of why vaccination worked came with the ______ theory of disease.
questions.explanation: 'That understanding came only with the germ theory of disease'.

questions.question: Who developed germ theory and extended vaccination to other diseases?
questions.explanation: 'the germ theory of disease, developed by Louis Pasteur... Pasteur extended the principle to other diseases'.

questions.question: In which year was smallpox declared eradicated worldwide?
questions.explanation: 'in 1980, when smallpox... was declared eradicated worldwide'.

passages.title: The Placebo Puzzle
passages.passage:
Few phenomena in medicine are as strange, or as revealing, as the placebo effect. A placebo is a treatment with no active ingredient — a sugar pill, a saline injection, a sham procedure — yet patients given placebos often report genuine improvement. For decades this was treated as a nuisance, a statistical noise that clinical trials had to filter out. More recently, researchers have begun to study the effect in its own right, and what they have found is challenging long-held assumptions about the boundary between mind and body.

The effect is real, but it is easy to overstate. Placebos do not shrink tumours or cure infections. What they can do is relieve symptoms that have a strong subjective component: pain, nausea, fatigue, anxiety. A patient who believes they are being treated may genuinely feel less pain, and brain scans confirm that placebos can trigger the release of the body's own painkilling chemicals. The improvement is not imaginary; it is a measurable physiological response set off by expectation.

Several factors influence how powerful a placebo will be. Perhaps surprisingly, the ritual surrounding a treatment matters as much as the treatment itself. A placebo injection tends to work better than a placebo pill, and an elaborate procedure better than a simple one. The colour of a pill, its price, even the confidence of the doctor administering it, all measurably affect the outcome. This suggests that much of the effect flows not from the substance but from the meaning the patient attaches to it.

The findings raise an awkward ethical question. If placebos work, should doctors prescribe them? The traditional objection is that doing so requires deception, and deceiving patients undermines trust. Yet a series of intriguing studies has found that placebos can work even when patients are told they are receiving a placebo — so-called open-label placebos. Why this should be so remains debated, but it hints that conscious belief is not the whole story; the body may respond to the ritual of treatment regardless.

Understanding the placebo effect matters for practical reasons. Every real treatment carries a placebo component alongside its active one, and the two are hard to disentangle. A drug that performs only slightly better than a placebo in trials may offer little genuine benefit. Conversely, learning to harness the placebo response ethically — through the manner in which care is delivered — could improve outcomes without any new drug at all. The placebo, once dismissed as a distraction, is turning out to be a window onto how healing actually works.

questions.question: A placebo contains no active ingredient.
questions.explanation: 'A placebo is a treatment with no active ingredient — a sugar pill, a saline injection, a sham procedure'.

questions.question: For many years, clinical trials tried to remove the placebo effect from their results.
questions.explanation: 'For decades this was treated as a nuisance, a statistical noise that clinical trials had to filter out'.

questions.question: Placebos can cure infections if the patient's belief is strong enough.
questions.explanation: 'Placebos do not shrink tumours or cure infections.'

questions.question: Which kinds of symptoms can placebos relieve?
options: symptoms with a strong subjective component
questions.answer: symptoms with a strong subjective component
questions.explanation: 'symptoms that have a strong subjective component: pain, nausea, fatigue, anxiety'.

questions.question: Brain scans show that placebos can trigger the release of the body's own ______ chemicals.
questions.explanation: 'placebos can trigger the release of the body's own painkilling chemicals'.

questions.question: According to the passage, a placebo injection generally works:
questions.explanation: 'A placebo injection tends to work better than a placebo pill'.

questions.question: The passage suggests much of the placebo effect comes from the ______ the patient attaches to the treatment.
questions.explanation: 'much of the effect flows not from the substance but from the meaning the patient attaches to it'.

questions.question: The main ethical objection to prescribing placebos is that it requires:
questions.explanation: 'doing so requires deception, and deceiving patients undermines trust'.

questions.question: Studies show placebos never work when patients know they are placebos.
questions.explanation: 'placebos can work even when patients are told they are receiving a placebo — so-called open-label placebos'.

questions.explanation: 'it hints that conscious belief is not the whole story; the body may respond to the ritual of treatment regardless'.

questions.question: A drug that beats a placebo only slightly in trials may offer little genuine ______.
questions.explanation: 'A drug that performs only slightly better than a placebo in trials may offer little genuine benefit'.

questions.question: The passage's overall view of the placebo effect is that it:
questions.explanation: 'The placebo... is turning out to be a window onto how healing actually works'.

questions.question: The passage states that the price of a pill can affect the placebo outcome.

passages.title: The Science of Sleep
passages.passage:
A. We spend roughly a third of our lives asleep, yet for most of human history sleep was regarded as little more than a passive shutdown, a nightly pause in the real business of living. That view has been overturned. Modern research reveals sleep as an intensely active state, during which the brain and body carry out essential work that cannot be done while we are awake.

B. Sleep is not uniform. Over a typical night we cycle several times through distinct stages, from light sleep into deep, slow-wave sleep and then into the phase known as REM, when the eyes dart about beneath closed lids and most dreaming occurs. Each stage appears to serve a different purpose, which is why disrupting sleep can be so harmful even when its total length is preserved.

C. One of sleep's most important jobs is the consolidation of memory. During the day the brain gathers a mass of new information; during sleep, it appears to sort through this material, strengthening the connections worth keeping and discarding the rest. Students who sleep after studying remember more than those who stay awake, and a full night's sleep before an exam may do more good than a night of frantic revision.

D. Sleep also serves the body. It is during deep sleep that growth hormone is released and tissues are repaired. The immune system, too, depends on adequate rest; people who sleep poorly are more susceptible to infection and recover more slowly. A striking recent discovery is that the brain has a kind of waste-clearance system that becomes far more active during sleep, flushing out proteins that accumulate during waking hours.

E. Given all this, it is troubling that many societies treat sleep as expendable. Artificial light, shift work, and the glow of screens have pushed bedtimes later and shortened sleep for millions. Chronic sleep deprivation has been linked to obesity, heart disease, depression, and impaired judgement. The economic costs of tired workers and drowsy drivers are enormous, though rarely counted.

F. The good news is that sleep responds to simple measures. Keeping regular hours, avoiding caffeine late in the day, and reducing screen use before bed all help. What does not help is the widespread belief that lost sleep can simply be 'caught up' at the weekend; while some recovery is possible, the evidence suggests it is incomplete. Sleep, it seems, is not a luxury to be rationed but a biological necessity as fundamental as food and water.

questions.question: Choose the best heading for Paragraph B:
questions.explanation: Paragraph B describes cycling through light, deep, and REM stages.

questions.question: Choose the best heading for Paragraph C:
questions.explanation: Paragraph C is about memory consolidation during sleep.

questions.question: Choose the best heading for Paragraph D:
questions.explanation: Paragraph D covers growth hormone, tissue repair, immunity, and brain waste clearance.

questions.question: Choose the best heading for Paragraph E:
options: The harm of too little sleep
questions.answer: The harm of too little sleep
questions.explanation: Paragraph E lists the harms of chronic sleep deprivation.

questions.explanation: 'for most of human history sleep was regarded as little more than a passive shutdown'.

questions.explanation: 'It is during deep sleep that growth hormone is released'.

questions.explanation: 'linked to obesity, heart disease, depression, and impaired judgement'.

passages.title: Rethinking the Doctor's Visit
passages.passage:
For more than a century, the model of medical care in most countries has been built around a single encounter: the patient, feeling unwell, visits the doctor, who diagnoses and prescribes. This model has achieved a great deal. But it was designed for a world in which the main threats to health were acute — infections, injuries, and sudden illnesses that struck, ran their course, and either killed or resolved. The diseases that dominate wealthy societies today are different, and the mismatch is becoming hard to ignore.

The chronic conditions that now account for most illness and death — heart disease, diabetes, many cancers — develop slowly, over years or decades. They are shaped less by single causes than by the accumulated effects of diet, exercise, stress, and environment. A fifteen-minute consultation, however skilled, is poorly suited to managing a disease that unfolds across a lifetime. Increasingly, doctors find themselves treating the late consequences of conditions that might have been prevented altogether.

This has prompted growing interest in what is sometimes called the shift 'upstream'. Rather than waiting for people to fall ill and then treating them, the argument runs, health systems should devote more effort to preventing illness in the first place. Some of this is medical: screening programmes, vaccination, the early detection of risk factors. But much of it lies outside the clinic altogether, in the conditions that shape how people live — housing, education, the availability of healthy food, the design of neighbourhoods that encourage or discourage physical activity.

The idea is not new, and it has always faced obstacles. Prevention is politically unrewarding: the benefits are diffuse, delayed, and invisible, whereas the cost of a new hospital is concrete and immediate. A treatment that saves an identifiable patient attracts far more attention than a policy that quietly prevents thousands from ever becoming patients. Health budgets, shaped by these pressures, remain overwhelmingly tilted towards treatment rather than prevention.

Technology may shift the balance. Wearable devices that monitor heart rate, activity, and sleep are putting continuous health data into ordinary hands, and could allow problems to be caught earlier. Yet enthusiasts sometimes overstate the case. Data alone changes nothing; what matters is whether it leads to action, and whether that action is available to everyone or only to the wealthy few who can afford the latest gadgets. There is a real risk that a technology sold as democratising health could instead widen the gap between those who can look after themselves and those who cannot.

None of this means abandoning the doctor's visit, which remains essential when people are genuinely ill. The argument is rather that a system built entirely around it is incomplete. The future of medicine, many now believe, lies not in the clinic alone but in the far messier business of shaping the conditions in which people live — a task that reaches well beyond anything a doctor can prescribe.

questions.question: The traditional model of care was designed for a world dominated by which kind of illness?
questions.explanation: 'it was designed for a world in which the main threats to health were acute — infections, injuries, and sudden illnesses'.

questions.explanation: 'A fifteen-minute consultation, however skilled, is poorly suited to managing a disease that unfolds across a lifetime'.

questions.question: The shift 'upstream' means focusing more on ______ illness rather than treating it.
questions.explanation: 'health systems should devote more effort to preventing illness in the first place'.

questions.question: Which of these is given as an example of prevention that lies outside the clinic?
questions.explanation: 'much of it lies outside the clinic altogether, in the conditions that shape how people live — housing, education, the availability of healthy food'.

questions.question: According to the passage, prevention is politically unrewarding because its benefits are diffuse, delayed and ______.
questions.explanation: 'the benefits are diffuse, delayed, and invisible'.

questions.question: Health budgets are currently tilted towards prevention rather than treatment.
questions.explanation: 'Health budgets... remain overwhelmingly tilted towards treatment rather than prevention'.

options: The promise and risk of technology
questions.answer: The promise and risk of technology

questions.explanation: 'a technology sold as democratising health could instead widen the gap between those who can look after themselves and those who cannot'.

questions.question: According to the passage, data on its own:
questions.explanation: 'Data alone changes nothing; what matters is whether it leads to action'.

questions.question: The passage argues that the doctor's visit should be abandoned.
questions.explanation: 'None of this means abandoning the doctor's visit, which remains essential when people are genuinely ill'.

questions.explanation: 'shaping the conditions in which people live'.

questions.question: Choose the best heading for the whole passage:
options: Why prevention deserves a bigger role in medicine
questions.answer: Why prevention deserves a bigger role in medicine
questions.explanation: The passage argues throughout for shifting effort 'upstream' towards prevention while keeping the doctor's visit.

task1.prompt: The chart below shows the number of hours per week that people in four different age groups spent doing physical exercise in a particular country in 2010 and 2022. Summarise the information by selecting and reporting the main features, and make comparisons where relevant. Write at least 150 words.

chartData.title: Average weekly hours of physical exercise by age group (2010 vs 2022)

task2.prompt: Some people believe that individuals are entirely responsible for their own health, while others argue that governments should do more to keep the population healthy. Discuss both views and give your own opinion. Give reasons for your answer and include any relevant examples from your own knowledge or experience. Write at least 250 words.

questions: Let's talk about staying healthy. What do you do to keep fit?
Has your idea of a healthy lifestyle changed as you've got older?
Do you prefer exercising indoors or outdoors? Why?
How important is a good night's sleep to you?
Do you think people in your country generally have healthy diets?

part2.cueCard: Describe a healthy habit you would like to develop. You should say: what the habit is; how you would go about developing it; what difficulties you might face; and explain how you think this habit would improve your life.
points: Preparation note: you have one minute to make notes before speaking for up to two minutes.
What the habit is and why you chose it
How you would develop it and what might get in the way
How it would improve your life

questions: Why do you think some people find it difficult to maintain healthy habits?
Whose responsibility should it be to keep people healthy — individuals, or the government?
How has technology changed the way people look after their health?
Do you think schools should do more to teach children about health? In what ways?
How might attitudes towards health change in the future?
